summary: 0x14062CA70 is now mapped as the timed wait helper used by the login/session root drain loops. Its RCX argument is a pointer to a signed millisecond duration; the drain callers place 0x64 in a stack local and pass the address, so the active request and pending-entry loops wait in 100 ms chunks while outstanding state remains live.
confidence: high for millisecond argument, QueryPerformanceCounter/QueryPerformanceFrequency usage, Sleep-backed wait loop, and 100 ms caller value; medium-high for the exact intermediate absolute-time conversion and clamp behavior.

behavior: Caches QueryPerformanceFrequency through 0x1407B4E10, with cached global 0x140C558C0.
Converts the counter to nanoseconds. The fast path for a 10,000,000 Hz counter multiplies by 100; the general path divides by frequency and multiplies the quotient/remainder by 1,000,000,000.
Reads *RCX. When the value is positive, multiplies it by 1,000,000 and adds it to the current nanosecond counter to form a deadline, saturating to INT64_MAX on overflow.
Loops until the current QueryPerformanceCounter-derived nanosecond value reaches the deadline.
While time remains, derives an absolute sleep target and calls 0x1407B5AF4, which uses KERNEL32!Sleep in a loop until the target time is reached.

evidence: Returns the qword value written to the stack local.
Calls KERNEL32.dll!QueryPerformanceFrequency through IAT 0x1408026E0 on first use and stores the result.
Calls 0x1407B7B48, which dispatches to KERNEL32.dll!GetSystemTimeAsFileTime when the optional runtime pointer is not installed.
Combines FILETIME high/low dwords and adds 0xFE624E212AC18000, the two's-complement subtraction of the Windows-to-Unix FILETIME epoch offset.
When EDX is 1 and RCX is non-null, calls 0x1407B4F08, divides the 100 ns epoch count by 10,000,000, and writes seconds at [RCX] and nanoseconds at [RCX+0x8].
Takes two seconds/nanoseconds-style structures, normalizes nanoseconds around 1,000,000,000, clamps negative deltas to zero, and returns a millisecond sleep interval.
Loops until current seconds/nanoseconds are greater than or equal to the requested target.

constants.role: Performance counter fast-path frequency and 100 ns ticks per second
Floating-point clamp/conversion constant used while deriving a sleep target
Integer form of the same large time clamp/conversion constant
Magic multiplier used to divide nanoseconds by 1,000,000,000 when splitting seconds and nanoseconds

opennxt_947_server_implications: The active request slot and pending-entry drains are explicitly timed waits, not tight spin loops. The client sleeps in roughly 100 ms poll chunks while request or entry state remains live.
Session close and root teardown can be delayed by outstanding OTL work. Server-side tests should avoid assuming immediate session/close emission or immediate root cleanup once teardown begins.
Because the wait is based on QueryPerformanceCounter but the sleep helper uses KERNEL32!Sleep, instrumentation may observe coarse 100 ms blocking behavior rather than per-frame polling.
The 0x64 stack value should be named as a millisecond duration in future field/callsite maps, not as an opaque scheduler token.

reverser_tool_update.why_it_matters: The wait helper used a raw qword constant at 0x140BAC240. Reverser now annotates finite little-endian double values for non-image qword constants, so time and math constants are visible directly in generated artifacts.
validation: Added float64 annotation support in pe_qwords.
Added pe-qwords schema coverage for float64.
Added a unit test for float64 constant annotation.

next_targets: Map the 0x78-byte pending-entry layout walked by 0x14062AB70 and 0x14062B0B0.
Resolve the indirect CALL R10 sites in 0x14062AB70 to identify close-request callback behavior.
Trace 0x14062BCE0 submit arguments from the session/close path.
Improve the lightweight x64 decoder around F2 48 0F 2A style CVTSI2SD instructions, which currently appear as DB fragments in the wait-helper artifact.
